behaviors.description: A file placed inside a subdirectory of the watched root is processed, not only files placed directly in the root directory.
behaviors.justification: The failure boundary is real filesystem watching behavior (recursive/child-path event delivery). In-process fakes can simulate child events but can miss OS-specific watcher semantics, so REAL-INFRA and MOCK-HIDE push this to System. Rejected Integration because behavior text is about actual watched-directory event behavior, not only internal routing logic.
behaviors.test_description: Run the daemon against a real temporary directory tree, create a nested subdirectory under the watched root, write a file there, and assert exactly one transformed output appears for that nested file path.

behaviors.description: The deduplication logic correctly discards a second processing request for the same file path within a configurable time window.
behaviors.justification: This behavior is pure dedupe decision logic for repeated requests on the same path/time window and does not inherently require OS/network/subprocess/LLM. MOCK-MISS check: a single-function/object test can catch real failures in keying/time-window comparison.
behaviors.test_description: Instantiate the dedupe component with a known window, submit the same file path twice within the window, assert first request accepted and second rejected; then advance time beyond the window and assert acceptance again.

behaviors.description: When the operating system emits multiple filesystem events for a single file write, the daemon processes the file exactly once.
behaviors.justification: The behavior explicitly depends on real OS event emission patterns for one write. Simulated duplicate callbacks may miss real watcher burst/timing behavior, so REAL-INFRA and MOCK-HIDE require System. Rejected Integration because the wording ties the failure to actual OS event behavior.
behaviors.test_description: With the daemon running on a real watched directory, perform a file write pattern known to produce multiple events on the host OS, then assert only one pipeline execution/output is produced for that file.

behaviors.description: If the watched directory is removed and then recreated while the daemon is running, the daemon resumes watching the new directory without a restart.
behaviors.justification: This is an OS/filesystem lifecycle condition (directory deletion/recreation, inode replacement, watcher re-subscription). In-process mocks can hide real watcher invalidation behavior, so System is the minimum real-failure boundary.
behaviors.test_description: Start daemon watching a real directory, delete that directory, recreate it at the same path, then add a new file and assert it is detected and processed without restarting the daemon.

behaviors.description: When multiple files arrive at the same time, they are processed in the order determined by their creation timestamps.
behaviors.justification: Ordering here depends on real file arrival plus filesystem timestamp behavior/precision under concurrent writes. A fake event source or fake metadata can always satisfy ordering and miss real timestamp/event-order edge cases, so System is needed.
behaviors.test_description: Create several files near-simultaneously in the real watched directory with controlled creation times, capture processing order from daemon logs/output metadata, and assert order matches creation timestamps.

behaviors.description: A file that is still being written to when the arrival event fires is not picked up for processing until writing is complete.
behaviors.justification: This is a real filesystem timing/partial-write condition. Mocked writer/watcher interactions can miss actual OS buffering/event timing, so mocking can hide failure and System is the minimum. No LLM or multi-agent steps involved.
behaviors.test_description: Begin a slow/streamed write to a file in the real watched directory, verify daemon does not process during active writes, finalize and close the file, then assert processing occurs afterward with complete contents.
